title: Phase mechanics: draft → specify → build → verify → done
when_to_read: Before any forward phase transition on a Spec, and before deciding which mode of `assess_spec` to call. The build-readiness rubric and narrative consolidation mechanics live here.
body:
## The five phases

`draft → specify → build → verify → done` is the forward pipeline. Each Spec lives in exactly one phase at a time, and the phase determines which actions are valid.

- **draft** — the Spec is being shaped. Decisions can be captured, narrative is in flux. No tasks yet (see non-negotiable rule #1).
- **specify** — open decisions get resolved here. Standards get referenced. The narrative becomes coherent. Move forward only when the build-readiness rubric returns clean.
- **build** — tasks exist, code gets written, tests get tagged with their AC. Most of the agent's day-to-day activity happens here.
- **verify** — all tasks complete, all Implementation ACs verified via test_events, narrative reflects what shipped. Before any PR that ships this work is opened, the relevant test harnesses must be GREEN — run them locally first; CI's required status checks are the enforcement backstop, not where you learn the suite is red. The user (not the agent) decides when to move from here.
- **done** — closed Spec. Read-only for normal operations.

Plus two orthogonal lifecycle flags, managed in the React UI (not via MCP):

- **paused** — work is suspended. Visible in lists, but not active.
- **archived** — historical. Hidden from default lists; opt in to see.

## Scope ACs come with the Spec — author them in draft / specify

The FIRST move after `create_doc` for a Spec in draft / specify is to author **Scope ACs**: plain-English, manager-authored outcome commitments that define what success looks like. They live alongside the Spec from the start and ground every downstream Decision.

- **When**: immediately after `create_doc`. Don't move on to framing Decisions until the spec carries its scope ACs (as many as genuinely capture what "done" means, usually three to six).
- **Who authors them**: the human / manager, with the agent's help. The agent's role is to walk the user through the question "what would 'this Spec succeeded' actually look like, in outcome terms?" and turn each answer into one Scope AC.
- **How**: `create_ac({ ref: '<spec-ref>', kind: 'scope', statement: '<the outcome commitment>' })`. Repeat for each.
- **Why this matters**: a Spec with no Scope ACs has no measurable success criteria. Decisions framed downstream lose their anchor — there's nothing for them to bottom out against. The audit trail at `done` becomes "we did stuff" instead of "we committed to outcomes X, Y, Z and here are the tests proving we delivered them."
- **Don't skip this**, even if the user doesn't ask. If you've just called `create_doc` and you're about to call `create_decision` without any Scope ACs in between, stop and ask: "What does success look like for this Spec? Let's get that down as Scope ACs first."

Implementation ACs are different — they're agent-spawned from resolved Decisions during specify / build, not authored up front. See `get_information(topic='ac-emission')` for the implementation-AC + test-tagging flow.

## `assess_spec` — the phase gate

`assess_spec` is the verb you call to learn whether a Spec is ready to move forward, and to perform the narrative-consolidation rituals that go with phase transitions. It has four modes:

- `assess_spec({mode:'phase', target:'<next-phase>'})` — the **build-readiness rubric** for the target phase. Returns a deterministic fact sheet (open decisions count, incomplete tasks, drift signals) plus a rubric that names what needs to be true. Walk the rubric against the facts, surface the verdict to the user. **Call this BEFORE any forward `update_doc({status:<phase>})`.** If the rubric isn't clean, the transition shouldn't happen.
- `assess_spec({mode:'narrative'})` — walks the changed decisions / sections with the user and updates the Spec narrative inline. Use at the specify → build transition to make the narrative reflect every resolved decision.
- `assess_spec({mode:'consolidate'})` — stamps the narrative anchor, marking the current narrative as consolidated. Run after `narrative` mode resolves the changes.
- `assess_spec({mode:'comments'})` — the standing open-comments survey. Useful at any transition to confirm there's no unresolved discussion blocking forward motion.

The specify → build gate is the most rigorous: the rubric demands that every resolved decision is reflected in the narrative AND that the narrative anchor is current. If either is stale, you can't enter build cleanly.

## Fetching the phase handoff prompt — `get_prompt`

Each working phase carries a handoff prompt: the full "do this phase" text a human pastes into a coding agent via the Spec page's copy-prompt button (specify → the plan handoff, build → the build handoff, verify → the verify handoff). `get_prompt({ ref: '<spec-ref>' })` returns that exact text over MCP — composed from the same scaffold node, with your Org's additions included — so there is no need to open the web UI to fetch it.

- It always returns the prompt for the Spec's CURRENT phase (no phase or button override). Call it after orienting on a Spec, or right after a phase transition, when the new phase's full instructions become relevant.
- `draft` and `done` carry no handoff prompt; the tool returns an explanation naming the phases that do, never an error.
- The short phase guidance you see in spec-tool response footers is the compressed projection of the same prompt; `get_prompt` is how you fetch the whole thing.

## Tool response shapes

Responses are **terse by default** — UUID + handle + state-transition hints, sized for tight tool-result budgets. Pass `verbose: true` on any tool to get the full markdown surface (doc state + formatters) when you genuinely need it right after a mutation. Default terse keeps the agent's context budget from getting eaten by repeated doc dumps.

Tool responses carry **Phase:** and **Allowed now:** hints at the top. Read them. The per-phase rules they cite are authoritative — if Allowed now: doesn't list the action you're about to take, it isn't allowed in this phase.

## The reverse direction

Moving a Spec backward (e.g. build → specify) happens when:

- A previously-resolved decision needs to be reopened (`update_decision({status:'open'})`). The Spec informally regresses; the rubric will catch it next time forward motion is attempted.
- A new decision surfaces mid-build (`create_decision`). Same effect — until resolved, build can't continue cleanly.

The agent doesn't manually call `update_doc({status:'specify'})` to step back; the decision-resolution state does the gating implicitly.
